original_abstract: When a person is diagnosed with cancer, making decisions about treatment is an overwhelming and complex process. In this chapter, we present an interdisciplinary project designed to automate the generation of personalized treatment descriptions for patients. Our approach integrates two extensive databases from the Netherlands Comprehensive Cancer Organisation (IKNL): the Netherlands Cancer Registry and the PROFILES dataset. By merging these resources, we were able to extract clinical data tailored to specific cancer types and individual profiles. To make this information more accessible and meaningful, we transformed raw statistics into personalized contexts using both verbal descriptions and narrative framing, with the goal of supporting shared decision-making between patients and clinicians. We conclude by evaluating the strengths and limitations of our methodology, discussing its potential for adaptation to other health domains, and reflecting on the broader implications of the research project.
humanized_abstract: Upon diagnosis with cancer, determining the most suitable treatment for their condition is a very difficult and complex decision for a cancer patient. The goal of this chapter is to present an interdisciplinary research project, which aims to computerize the production of individualized treatment summaries that can support shared decision making between a patient and physician. To this end, we linked two large sources from the Netherlands Comprehensive Cancer Organisation (IKNL) – the Netherlands Cancer Registry and the PROFILES dataset – and used information on individualized treatment per cancer site and patient characteristic to create insight in cancer treatment for patients with a particular cancer type and profile. To create the actual summaries that support SDM, we converted the data into more human readable format including both prose explanations as well as narrative orientations. We reflect on the applicability and the limitations of the research approach we adopted, and we hypothesize how the work can be extended towards other health areas, and that it reflects on the underlying value of the research project.
